---
title: "What the colors session resolved, deferred, and left open for the schema"
date: 2026-03-26
categories: [ifid, colors, data-analysis, track-a]
author:
  - "Lalitha A R"
  - "Claude (Anthropic)"
description: "44 non-INS color variant rows processed → 30 INS colour entries in the clean taxonomy, 7 whole-food colorants deferred. The 7 deferred entries expose a category gap in IFID: substances that function as colorants on Indian labels but have no INS number and no current home in the schema."
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

## What this session did

The colors session started from two inputs:

- 25 INS colour entries already in the additives taxonomy, exported to
  `colors_ins_clean.csv` as a working reference
- 44 non-INS colour variant rows from `core/all_variants_working.csv` — declarations
  found on Indian labels that had not yet been matched to an INS number

Of the 44 variant rows: 37 were resolved and removed from the working file. 5 new
INS entries were added to the taxonomy. 7 rows were deferred.

Session close: 2026-03-13.

---

## The 30 INS colour entries

`colors_ins_clean.csv` holds 30 INS colour entries. This is the reference file for
downstream colour resolution work.

Source type distribution across the 30:

| source_type | Count |
|---|---|
| plant | 16 |
| synthetic | 11 |
| mineral | 3 |

The INS colour range in Codex runs roughly 100–174. All 30 entries fall within
this range, with the exception of INS 152 (Polyethylene glycol, listed as a colour
adjunct) and the caramel sub-types (150a, 150c, 150d) which share the 150 base
number.

Full list by INS number:

| INS | Official name | source_type |
|---|---|---|
| 100 | Curcumin | plant |
| 101(i) | Riboflavin | synthetic |
| 102 | Tartrazine | synthetic |
| 110 | Sunset yellow FCF, Orange yellow S | synthetic |
| 122 | Azorubine, Carmoisine | synthetic |
| 124 | Ponceau 4R | synthetic |
| 127 | Erythrosine | synthetic |
| 129 | Allura Red AC | synthetic |
| 132 | Indigotine, Indigo carmine | synthetic |
| 133 | Brilliant Blue FCF | synthetic |
| 140 | Chlorophylls | plant |
| 141 | Copper complexes of chlorophylls | plant |
| 150 | Caramel | plant |
| 150a | Plain caramel | plant |
| 150c | Ammonia caramel | plant |
| 150d | Sulphite ammonia caramel | plant |
| 152 | Polyethylene glycol | synthetic |
| 160a | Carotenes, mixed | plant |
| 160a(i) | Beta-carotene, vegetable | plant |
| 160b | Annatto extracts | plant |
| 160c | Paprika extract | plant |
| 160d | Lycopene | plant |
| 161b | Lutein | plant |
| 161g | Canthaxanthin | synthetic |
| 162 | Beetroot Red | plant |
| 163 | Anthocyanins | plant |
| 163c | Grape skin extract | plant |
| 170 | Calcium carbonate | mineral |
| 171 | Titanium dioxide | mineral |
| 174 | Silver | mineral |

---

## How the 37 rows were resolved

### Pass 1 — Match to existing INS (25 rows, no new entries)

25 variant rows mapped to INS entries already in the taxonomy. These included
FD&C-named synthetic dyes (e.g. "FD&C Red 40" → INS 129 Allura Red AC) and
botanical extract declarations where the INS already existed. No new taxonomy
entries were required.

Distribution of resolved variants by INS:

INS 100 (×2), 102 (×2), 110 (×3), 129 (×6), 132 (×2), 133 (×3), 160a(i) (×1),
160b (×3), 160c (×2), 171 (×1).

### Pass 2 — INS exists in Codex, absent from taxonomy (6 rows, 4 new entries)

Four entries added: INS 140 (Chlorophylls), 160d (Lycopene), 161b (Lutein), and
150 (Caramel, as a single undifferentiated entry alongside the existing sub-types
150a, 150c, 150d). All four are plant-source entries.

One taxonomy addition not counted here: INS 174 (Silver, mineral) was added in
Pass 4 below.

### Pass 3 — Unresolvable group declarations (5 rows dropped)

`artificial color`, `artificial colors`, `synthetic food colors`, `color vegetable
juice`, `vegetable juice concentrate for color` — no single INS number is resolvable
from any of these declarations. They were removed from the working file and not added
to the taxonomy.

### Pass 4 — INS absent from taxonomy, mineral source (1 row, 1 entry added)

`silver leaf` → INS 174 (Silver, mineral). Added to taxonomy.

---

## The 7 deferred rows and the schema gap

7 rows were not resolved to an INS number. They remain in `core/all_variants_working.csv`
with `f_revised` set to `colour|raw_agricultural_material`. They will be processed
during a raw agricultural material session that has not yet run.

| Label text | Source | Note |
|---|---|---|
| kesar | saffron | Hindi name; variant pair with `safron` |
| safron | saffron | Spelling variant; variant pair with `kesar` |
| paprika | paprika | Whole dried spice; distinct from INS 160c (solvent-extracted oleoresin) |
| hibiscus | hibiscus | Dried flower |
| carrot powder | carrot | Dried whole carrot; distinct from INS 160a(i) beta-carotene extract |
| carrot flakes | carrot | Dried whole carrot |
| pomegranate powder | pomegranate | Dried whole fruit powder |

### Why these were deferred

Each of these appears on Indian labels as a colour declaration — the product label
uses one of these names in the context of declaring colour. None has an INS number.
The deferral is not a classification decision; it is an acknowledgement that the
current taxonomy structure has no place for them.

These substances share a structural property: they are dried, powdered, or extracted
forms of whole food ingredients used for their colouring effect. They are not
additives (no INS number, not in the Codex food additives framework). They are not
raw ingredients in the conventional IFID sense — they are present on the label as
colour declarations, not as primary ingredients.

The `raw_agricultural_material` lane referenced in their `f_revised` field does not
exist in IFID. It was identified during this session as the correct home for these
entries; building that category is a separate task.

### The distinction paprika/carrot illustrate

INS 160c is "Paprika extract" — a solvent-extracted oleoresin from paprika, with an
INS number, classified as a synthetic colour in Codex terms. The paprika row in the
deferred set is the whole dried spice ground to a powder.

INS 160a(i) is "Beta-carotene, vegetable" — an extract from carrot. The carrot
powder and carrot flakes rows are dried whole carrot.

These are distinct substances with distinct regulatory status. The INS extract has
a Codex classification. The whole-food form has no INS number and no Codex colour
classification. Both appear on Indian labels as colour declarations. The taxonomy
resolves the extract; it cannot resolve the whole-food form.

---

## Regional naming: kesar vs saffron

`kesar` is the Hindi name for saffron. It appears on Indian labels where an
international-market label would say `saffron`. The two variant rows (`kesar`,
`safron`) resolve to the same source substance.

`safron` is a spelling variant — not the correct English spelling, not the correct
Hindi spelling, but a form found on labels.

This is a labeling normalisation problem, not a taxonomy gap. The deferred entry
is not "kesar" as a distinct substance from saffron; it is saffron appearing under
its Hindi name. Any IFID matching pipeline processing Indian labels needs to handle
regional-language ingredient names alongside English and INS declarations.

The Hindi naming issue is not specific to colours. It is visible here because the
colors session processed label text variants directly. The broader implication —
that Indian labels use Hindi names for ingredients with no INS number — surfaces
as a design question for IFID's ingredient text matching layer.

---

## Design question: INS number, common name, or both?

The 30 INS colour entries each carry an `official_name`. For plant-source and
synthetic entries, the official name is the Codex GSFA name — which may not be the
name on the label.

Examples:

- INS 129: official name is "Allura Red AC." Indian labels may say "Allura Red,"
  "Red 40," "FD&C Red 40," or in some cases just "E129" or "INS 129."
- INS 100: official name is "Curcumin." Indian labels commonly say "turmeric colour"
  or "हल्दी" (haldi).
- INS 160b: official name is "Annatto extracts." Labels may say "annatto," "bixin,"
  "norbixin," or "natural colour (annatto)."

The 25 Pass 1 resolutions were precisely this problem — variant label text mapped
to canonical INS entries. The mapping is in `core/all_variants_working.csv` (resolved
rows removed) and in the Pass 1 INS distribution above, but it is not currently
captured as a structured alias list in the taxonomy.

Whether IFID displays INS number, official name, common names, or all three for a
colour entry is a UI question. Whether IFID stores label text aliases per entry is
a schema question. The current taxonomy has the INS number and official name;
it does not have a `label_aliases` or `common_names` field.

---

## Data files

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `colors/colors_ins_clean.csv` | 30 INS colour entries; reference for downstream colour resolution |
| `colors/colors_nonins_variants.csv` | 7 deferred whole-food rows; input for raw_agricultural_material session |
| `colors/cp/README_colors.md` | Session close notes; full resolution log, taxonomy additions |
| `additives/additives_taxonomy.json` | Source of truth for all INS entries; updated with 5 additions during this session |

---

## Note on AI authorship

This document was drafted by Claude (Anthropic, Sonnet 4.6) in a Claude Code session
on 2026-03-26, from a structured plan written by Lalitha A R.

**What Claude did:**

- Queried `colors_ins_clean.csv` to confirm 30 entries and source type distribution
- Identified the 5 entries added in the colors session from the README and confirmed
  them in the CSV
- Read the full session README to verify the four-pass resolution log and deferred
  row details
- Drafted all prose, tables, and section structure

**Constraints Claude worked under:**

- The paprika/carrot INS-versus-whole-food distinction is drawn from the README
  notes on the deferred rows; it is not an inference.
- The kesar/saffron Hindi naming observation is from the README; the broader
  implication for IFID's ingredient text matching is Claude's synthesis.
- The label aliases design question is raised from the Pass 1 data — 25 variant
  forms mapped to 10 INS numbers — not from the plan.
- No claims were made about FSSAI labeling requirements for colour declarations.
  The absence of a regulatory statement is not an oversight; the session did not
  produce regulatory data on colour declarations.
